1: A shadow: the command to guard one's steps approaching the house of God — to come near to listen rather than to sacrifice carelessly — anticipates the one who replaces the temple entirely (John 2:19–21). Jesus, the true temple and the one mediator (1 Timothy 2:5), is the destination that all temple-approach imagery points toward. The caution against the sacrifice of fools is answered in Christ's one sufficient sacrifice (Hebrews 10:14).
2: The vast asymmetry between the creature and God — "God is in heaven and you are on earth; let your words be few" — defines the problem of access that the Incarnation resolves. Christ, who is both in heaven and on earth, the eternal Word who speaks from the Father's side (John 1:18), is the one in whom the creature's words are received and the divine word fully given. The theme of divine transcendence here points toward the need for a mediator.
3: The revelation of God: even the governance of words and dreams reflects the order of a God who sees through human noise to the heart. This wisdom teaching is part of the larger OT picture of God's sovereign discernment — which the NT presents as fully expressed in Christ, who knows what is in a person and needs no testimony (John 2:25).
4: The theme: vow-keeping before God reflects the covenantal seriousness of the God who holds his people to their word — and who himself keeps every covenant promise. The NT presents this fulfillment in Christ, in whom all the promises of God find their "Yes" (2 Corinthians 1:20). The instruction not to delay in fulfilling vows points toward the one who fulfilled every obligation perfectly on behalf of a people who could not.
5: The theme: better not to make a vow than to vow and not fulfill — a principle of integrity before God. Christ embodies total integrity and the fulfillment of every commitment; in him, the gap between promise and performance that mars human religion is closed.
6: The theme: the mouth that leads into sin and invites divine anger names the problem of human speech before God. Christ is the Word who speaks only what the Father gives (John 12:49) and whose words do not bring judgment but life — and whose intercessory work covers the failures of his people's speech before God.
7: The theme: "Fear God" — the closing word after surveying the futility of many dreams and words — is the Wisdom tradition's fundamental orientation, one that the NT presents as properly directed toward the one in whom God is fully revealed. The fear of God that begins wisdom (Proverbs 1:7) is the fear that, in the NT, takes the form of reverent submission to Christ as Lord (Philippians 2:10–11).
8: The revelation of God: oppression perpetuated through hierarchies of officials — each covered by a higher — is one face of the fallen order that cries out for a judge above all earthly authorities. Christ as the Lord of lords and Judge of the living and dead (Acts 10:42) stands over every human hierarchy and will vindicate the oppressed poor (James 5:4).
9: The theme: even kings benefit from cultivated land — even the greatest earthly power is bound to the created order and dependent on what the earth yields. This leveling observation participates in the OT wisdom theme of created dependence, which the NT presents as the frame within which Christ the Lord chose to enter as one who ate and drank and was hungry — taking his place within the creaturely order he sustains.
10: The theme: the insatiability of the lover of money — never satisfied with wealth — names the disordered desire that Ecclesiastes traces throughout. Christ's teaching names mammon as the rival to God (Matthew 6:24), and Paul declares contentment in all circumstances as possible only "through Christ who strengthens me" (Philippians 4:13). The vapor of wealth is answered by "true riches" (Luke 16:11).
11: The theme: as goods multiply, so do those who consume them — wealth grows a following but not a self. This observation about the futility of accumulation points toward the "better things" (Hebrews 11:16) that do not multiply consumption but multiply participation in the divine life; Christ promises treasure that cannot be consumed or taken away (Matthew 6:20).
12: The theme: the sweet sleep of the laborer against the sleepless anxiety of the wealthy restless — wealth that brings no rest names the fallen condition in which human security is placed in the wrong thing. Christ offers the rest that wealth cannot purchase (Matthew 11:28–30); the one who trusts him can sleep in peace (Psalms 4:8).
13: The revelation of God: wealth hoarded at the owner's own expense — a grievous evil observed under the sun — participates in the OT critique of false security in possessions. The NT presents Christ's parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16–21) as the sharp edge of this Ecclesiastes observation, and Paul warns that those who desire to be rich fall into temptation (1 Timothy 6:9). Hoarding rather than holding loosely is the diagnostic of a heart not anchored in Christ.
14: The theme: riches lost in a bad venture and a son with nothing — the nakedness of failed inheritance. The fathers who cannot pass wealth to their children reflect the universal limitation of earthly inheritance, against which stands Christ who has secured an inheritance "imperishable, undefiled, and unfading" for those born again into his family (1 Peter 1:4).
15: A shadow: "As he came from his mother's womb, so he will leave — naked." This most universal of limits — one cannot take anything from this life — is what Christ assumes and transforms. He entered the world in poverty (2 Corinthians 8:9) and left it on a cross, having taken nothing material. But through his resurrection he carries humanity into permanent participation in the divine life — the one who was naked on the cross now clothes his people in righteousness (Revelation 3:18).
16: The theme: laboring for the wind — the nihilistic possibility that effort produces nothing lasting — is precisely what the resurrection forecloses. Paul draws on exactly this Ecclesiastes register in 1 Corinthians 15:58: "Your labor in the Lord is not in vain" — the antidote to laboring for wind is laboring in the one who has overcome death and promises his work will stand.
17: The revelation of God: eating in darkness, burdened with sickness, vexation, and anger — life consumed by its own weight — names the groaning condition that Paul names in Romans 8:22. Christ bore this darkness in Gethsemane and on the cross; his resurrection is the first movement of creation's own liberation from this bondage (Romans 8:21).
18: The theme: Qohelet's "good and fitting" — to eat and drink and find enjoyment in one's labor as one's God-given portion — is a limited but real good. The NT affirms the goodness of created enjoyment (1 Timothy 4:3–4) but locates the permanent version of this gift in Christ; Paul's "whatever you do, do it to the glory of God" (1 Corinthians 10:31) transforms the portioned enjoyment of Ecclesiastes into participation in the life of the age to come.
19: The theme: when God gives someone the capacity to enjoy wealth and labor — "that is a gift of God." The ability to receive creation's goods as gift rather than anxiety is itself a grace, one that the NT anchors in knowing the Giver. Christ, in whom God's generosity is fully expressed (James 1:17), is the one in whom his people learn contentment and enjoyment as a form of worship (Philippians 4:11–12).
20: The theme: God keeping the heart occupied with joy so that one does not brood over fleeting years — the divine gift of present-moment joy. Christ embodies and mediates this joy: "These things I have spoken to you so that my joy may be in you and your joy may be complete" (John 15:11). The brooding over transience that Ecclesiastes diagnoses is answered in the one who says "I am the resurrection and the life" (John 11:25).
